title: SEGMENT 6 — Jumping genes — SVA retrotransposons, the "junk DNA" that wasn't junk

lines.text: SEGMENT 6 — Jumping genes — SVA retrotransposons, the "junk DNA" that wasn't junk
So let me tell you about SVAs, because these things are weird and wonderful and they say something interesting about what your genome actually is.
An SVA stands for SINE-VNTR-Alu.
That's an acronym made of acronyms, which is unfortunate.

SINE means "short interspersed nuclear element," VNTR means "variable number of tandem repeats," and Alu refers to a particular family of repetitive sequences named after a restriction enzyme cut site within them.

The name is a Frankenstein because the element itself is a Frankenstein — a hybrid composite of three different repetitive DNA elements that got fused together somewhere in primate evolutionary history into a single chimeric mobile element.
SVAs are unique to great apes; you find them in humans, chimps, gorillas, orangutans, but not in monkeys further down the primate tree, not in other mammals.
They are evolutionarily recent.
There are perhaps three thousand of them scattered throughout the human genome.
But the deeper context you need to know is that an SVA is one example of a much larger phenomenon called transposable elements or retrotransposons or, casually, jumping genes.
Roughly half of the human genome — fifty percent — is made up of retrotransposon-derived sequence.
Half.
Of your DNA.
Is the result of a particular kind of molecular parasite that has been spending hundreds of millions of years copying itself around inside the genomes of your ancestors.
Let me give you the analogy.
A retrotransposon is a parasitic Word macro.
Imagine you have a document — a long, important text document — and there is a macro inside it whose only job is to find itself and paste a copy of itself somewhere else in the document, at random.
Every time the macro runs, the document gets a little longer, with one more copy of the macro spliced in at a new location.
Over time, if you run the macro enough times, the document fills up with copies of the macro.

The original text is still in there somewhere, but it is increasingly surrounded by, and in some cases interrupted by, copies of the parasitic macro that has been replicating itself for as long as the document has existed.
That is, more or less, what has happened to your genome.
The vast bulk of the three billion bases you carry around is not genes.
It is not regulatory sequence.

It is repetitive copies of mobile elements — LINEs and SINEs and SVAs and endogenous retroviruses — that have, over evolutionary time, copied themselves and pasted themselves into your DNA, and into your ancestors' DNA, over and over and over.
Most of these copies are now defunct, broken, immobile.
They cannot copy themselves anymore.
They are, in evolutionary terms, fossils — the wreckage of past invasions.
But some, a small minority, are still active.
They can still copy themselves.
And when they do, they paste somewhere new in the genome, occasionally — rarely — in a place where they disrupt a gene.
How do they copy themselves, mechanistically?
Retrotransposons replicate through an RNA intermediate.
The DNA copy of the retrotransposon gets transcribed into RNA.

The RNA gets reverse-transcribed back into DNA, by a reverse transcriptase enzyme that the retrotransposon itself encodes — or, in the case of SVAs, by an enzyme borrowed from another retrotransposon called L1.
That new DNA copy is then inserted back into the genome, often at a new location.
The original copy is unchanged.
There is now one more copy in the genome than there was before.
Over generations, copies accumulate.

The reason about half of the human genome is retrotransposon-derived is that this process has been going on for an extremely long time, the active elements have inserted millions of copies, and natural selection generally cannot remove a retrotransposon insertion that does not cause overt harm.
Most insertions are harmless — they land in non-coding sequence and don't disrupt anything.
They get inherited along with everything else.
So the count just goes up over evolutionary time.
Your genome is, in a real sense, mostly an archaeological site of past retrotransposon activity.
For most of the twentieth century, biologists referred to all this material as junk DNA.

The thinking was that since it was repetitive, since it didn't code for protein, since it had no obvious function, it must be useless filler — junk that the genome was carrying around for no good reason.
That view has shifted considerably in the last twenty years.
Some retrotransposon-derived sequences have been co-opted by the genome for regulatory functions; some have evolved into novel genes; some seem to play roles in chromosome structure or recombination.
The "junk DNA" framing was always too dismissive.
But for the most part, your retrotransposon load is genuinely doing nothing useful in your day-to-day biology.
It's just there.
Most of the time.

But sometimes, the retrotransposon machinery is still active enough in human cells — particularly in germ cells, the cells that produce sperm and eggs — that a new insertion occurs, and that new insertion is then passed to the next generation.
Most new insertions, again, are harmless.
But occasionally one lands in a gene and disrupts it.
And when that happens, you have a new genetic disease, born in that one generation, often unique to that one family, sometimes unique to that one person.
This is what happened in Mila's case.

At some point — perhaps in her parents, perhaps a generation or two earlier, the timing is impossible to know precisely — an active SVA somewhere in the genome was transcribed, reverse-transcribed, and pasted into intron 6 of MFSD8 on one chromosome.
That insertion was harmless in the carrier who first received it, because they still had a normal copy of MFSD8 on their other chromosome.
The insertion was passed down.
It eventually reached one of Mila's parents, who passed it to Mila.
From her other parent, Mila received the c.1102G>C point mutation.
Now she had no working copy of MFSD8 at all.
And the disease unfolded.

The reason this matters for our story — beyond just the elegance of the molecular detective work — is that an SVA insertion in an intron is exactly the kind of mutation that is hardest to find with standard testing, hardest to interpret when you do find it, and, as we are about to see, easiest to fix with a specifically targeted ASO.
Because what the SVA actually did, mechanistically, inside the MFSD8 gene, is a problem with a precise molecular shape — and that precise shape is something we can design a drug to mask.
Which is the topic of Segment 7.
